chunks.content_preview: ## Introduction **Patrick:** My guest today is Shyam Sankar, the CTO of Palantir Technologies. I think you'll find by listening to Shyam that the business itself, their mission and how they built i…

propositions.content: Shyam Sankar is the CTO of Palantir Technologies, and his life and worldview are fascinating.
propositions.source_text: My guest today is Shyam Sankar, the CTO of Palantir Technologies. I think you'll find by listening to Shyam that the business itself, their mission and how they built it, and indeed him and his life, are completely fascinating.

propositions.content: The conversation covers the need to reindustrialize the United States after decades of offshoring production.
propositions.source_text: This need to reindustrialize the United States after decades of moving so much of our production and as he would argue, therefore our learning of how to innovate and make things, off of our own shores.

propositions.content: The discussion includes the history of heretics who have designed solutions that contributed to global success.
propositions.source_text: We talk about the history of heretics, people who have against the odds designed solutions that have driven so much of our success around the world.

claims.content: Shyam Sankar's worldview is personally interesting to Patrick.
claims.source_text: I find personally the most interesting.

claims.content: The United States has moved much of its production off of its own shores.

This need to reindustrialize the United States after decades of moving so much of our production and as he would argue, therefore our learning of how to innovate and make things, off of our own shores.

chunks.content_preview: Please enjoy my conversation with Shyam Sankar. ## Great Heretics Through History **Patrick:** Shyam, I think the most fun place for me to begin, because it's an area that you're interested in that…

propositions.content: Shyam Sankar discusses American military figures he refers to as heretics, including Hyman Rickover and Andrew Higgins, who designed 90% of the boats that landed at Normandy.
propositions.source_text: Shyam, I think the most fun place for me to begin, because it's an area that you're interested in that I'm fascinated by, is a history of people in American military lore who you call heretics. You refer to them as heretics, people like Hyman Rickover and Andrew Higgins, who designed 90% of the boats that landed at Normandy.

propositions.content: John Boyd invented the OODA loop, which is considered one of the great biographies of a military figure.
propositions.source_text: John Boyd, who invented the OODA loop, which is one of the great biographies of a military figure that I've ever read.

propositions.content: Shyam Sankar defines heretics as founder figures in US history.
propositions.source_text: Well, I think they're really founders. They're founder figures.

claims.content: Hyman Rickover and Andrew Higgins designed 90% of the boats at Normandy.
claims.source_text: You refer to them as heretics, people like Hyman Rickover and Andrew Higgins, who designed 90% of the boats that landed at Normandy.

claims.content: The OODA loop was invented by John Boyd.
claims.source_text: John Boyd, who invented the OODA loop, which is one of the great biographies of a military figure that I've ever read.

claims.content: Shyam Sankar is interested in heretics in US history.
claims.source_text: Talk to us a little bit about how you would define a heretic in your use of that term, and why you're so interested in that group of people in US history.

chunks.content_preview: They're founder figures. They get obsessed with delivering something that, it makes no sense because they're fighting, particularly in the military context, you're like fighting the bureaucracy. Yo…

propositions.content: Founder figures are obsessed with delivering something despite fighting bureaucracy, which can end their careers.
propositions.source_text: They're founder figures. They get obsessed with delivering something that, it makes no sense because they're fighting, particularly in the military context, you're like fighting the bureaucracy. You're going to end your career.

propositions.content: Great founders exhibit a pathological obsession with winning, which the speaker relates to personally.
propositions.source_text: So there's almost like a pathological obsession with winning, which I really relate to both personally, but also that's what I see in great founders.

propositions.content: The speaker believes that change comes from hidden heretics in the military who later become heroes.
propositions.source_text: In the present moment, it's really important to encourage the hidden heretics who are in the military today to recognize your heresy matters.

claims.content: The speaker sees a connection between heretics and successful outcomes in wars.
claims.source_text: And frankly, if you look back at history, the only shit that ever worked, the things that helped us win all the wars, were the things that the heretics actually did.

claims.content: Nothing that went through the bureaucracy delivered successful outcomes.
claims.source_text: Nothing that went through the machine delivered anything.

chunks.content_preview: And they only later become heroes. If you think about Billy Mitchell, who invented the Air Force, he was court-martialed and died penniless and depressed. And only after his death did we invent the…

propositions.content: Billy Mitchell invented the Air Force, was court-martialed, and died penniless and depressed.
propositions.source_text: If you think about Billy Mitchell, who invented the Air Force, he was court-martialed and died penniless and depressed.

propositions.content: The Air Force was invented based on Billy Mitchell's original heresy after his death.
propositions.source_text: And only after his death did we invent the Air Force based on his original heresy.

chunks.content_preview: S. when he was six, had one of these near-miss moments where, in those days when you landed at Ellis Island, you had 10 days for someone to come pick you up. His mother gave someone money to go sen…

propositions.content: S. had a near-miss moment when he was six years old at Ellis Island, where he had 10 days for someone to come pick him up.
propositions.source_text: S. when he was six, had one of these near-miss moments where, in those days when you landed at Ellis Island, you had 10 days for someone to come pick you up.

propositions.content: S.'s mother gave someone money to send a telegram to his father, who had already been in the United States.
propositions.source_text: His mother gave someone money to go send a telegram to the father who'd already been here.

propositions.content: On the 10th day, another man arrived at Ellis Island and helped S. by buying them one more day and retrieving his father.
propositions.source_text: On the 10th day, there was another guy who arrived at Ellis Island who bought them one more day, who knew them.

propositions.content: S. was a feisty character who was five foot two and attended the Naval Academy.
propositions.source_text: But he was this feisty character. I think he was five foot two, very short. He went to the Naval Academy.

propositions.content: S. was so unlikable at the Naval Academy that they tore out his picture from the yearbook.
propositions.source_text: He was so unlikable that in the yearbook in the Naval Academy, they had torn out his picture.

claims.content: S. would have been sent back to Poland if not for the help he received.
claims.source_text: And then he ran out, got the father, because it was this near-miss where this guy almost didn't even end up here, he would have been sent back to Poland.

claims.content: The man who helped S. on the 10th day knew S. and his family.
claims.source_text: who bought them one more day, who knew them.

claims.content: S. was described as very short at five foot two.
claims.source_text: I think he was five foot two, very short.

claims.content: S.'s picture was removed from the Naval Academy yearbook due to his unlikable nature.
claims.source_text: they had torn out his picture.

propositions.content: There have been no deaths due to nuclear incidents in the submarine force.
propositions.source_text: We've had no deaths due to nuclear incidents in our submarine force.

propositions.content: The designer of the submarine created it to a high safety standard, believing it to be a hundred times safer than the minimum standard.
propositions.source_text: And he designed it to a standard. He's like, I'm designing this thing so my son could be in it. It's a hundred times safer than what we believe the minimum standard to be.

propositions.content: Hyman Rickover was considered one of the Navy's three enemies by Zumwalt, who was the chief of naval operations.
propositions.source_text: Zumwalt, who was the chief of naval operations for a while, he said "The Navy has three enemies, the Soviet Union, the Air Force, and Hyman Rickover. "

chunks.content_preview: " ## Channeling Disagreeableness **Patrick:** I remember reading the book about John Boyd, who – three times, he was the best fighter pilot. Then he helped develop the F-16, and then he came up wit…

propositions.content: John Boyd was the best fighter pilot three times, helped develop the F-16, and created a military strategy concept that was instrumental in some U.S. wars in the nineties.
propositions.source_text: I remember reading the book about John Boyd, who – three times, he was the best fighter pilot. Then he helped develop the F-16, and then he came up with the military strategy concept that was instrumental in some of the U. S. wars in the nineties.

propositions.content: John Boyd was highly disliked, a really bad father, and highly disagreeable, but incredibly important in U.S. military history.
propositions.source_text: And he was sort of a bastard as well. Highly disliked, really bad father, highly disagreeable, but incredibly important in U. S. military history.

propositions.content: The speaker is perceived as very likable within Palantir and outside of Palantir.
propositions.source_text: You seem, by all accounts, very likable within Palantir and outside of Palantir.

claims.source_text: You don't seem to have that kind of personality.
